content.text:
# Ranking metrics for recommenders

Ranking metrics answer the question that actually matters in production: **"how good is the top-K list of items I'm about to show this user?"** Most users see a list, not a score, so in practice these dominate the older [[reco-rating-metrics|rating metrics]] like RMSE.

The setup is simple. For each user $u$:

- $R_u$ = the top-K items the model recommends
- $T_u$ = the items that are actually relevant for $u$ (held-out positives from the test set)

There are four metrics worth knowing. The first two are about *what's in the set*; the last two add *where it sits in the ranking*.

## Precision@K and Recall@K — set-level

$$\text{Precision@K} = \frac{|R_u \cap T_u|}{K}, \qquad \text{Recall@K} = \frac{|R_u \cap T_u|}{|T_u|}$$

**Precision@K** asks: *of the K things I recommended, how many were correct?* Denominator is fixed at K, so it tells you about the quality of what made the list.

**Recall@K** asks: *of all the relevant items that exist, how many did I surface in the top-K?* Denominator is the total number of relevant items, so it tells you about coverage of the user's true interests.

These trade off against each other. For small K (think K = 10 in a Netflix shelf), **precision dominates the user experience** — nobody cares that you missed 200 other relevant items if the 10 you showed are great. For a candidate-generation step ahead of a reranker (the **2-step architecture** from this lecture), recall is what matters because you want the reranker to have all the good candidates available.

Crucially, both metrics are **set-based** — they don't care whether the hit is at rank 1 or rank 10, as long as it's in the top-K. That's the gap NDCG and MAP fill.

## MAP@K — position-weighted precision

For one user, **Average Precision** is:

$$\text{AP@K} = \frac{1}{|T_u|} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Precision@}k \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{item at position } k \in T_u]$$

Read it as: walk down the list from rank 1 to K, and every time you hit a relevant item, record the running Precision@k *at that position*. Average those records and divide by the total number of relevant items.

The trick is that an early hit produces a higher partial Precision@k (because the denominator is small), so **a hit at rank 1 contributes a lot more than a hit at rank K**. Average AP@K across users → **MAP@K**.

## NDCG@K — ranking quality with a logarithmic discount

NDCG is the metric most papers report when they want to claim "we rank well." Two-step definition:

$$\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{2^{\text{rel}_k} - 1}{\log_2(k+1)}, \qquad \text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$$

- $\text{rel}_k$ is the relevance score at rank $k$ (binary 0/1 in implicit-feedback settings; graded 1–5 with explicit ratings)
- The numerator $2^{\text{rel}_k} - 1$ makes higher relevance count exponentially more
- The denominator $\log_2(k+1)$ **discounts items further down the list** — that's where "ranking" enters the metric
- IDCG is the DCG of the *ideal* ordering (all relevant items at the top, sorted by relevance), so NDCG ∈ [0, 1] regardless of how many relevant items the user actually has

Why log₂? It's a smooth, slow decay — rank 1 gets full weight (log₂(2) = 1), rank 7 gets ~⅓, rank 31 gets ~⅕. Punishes "the right answer at rank 8" without being as harsh as a 1/k discount.

## Quick comparison

| Metric | Cares about hits? | Cares about position? | Single number per user? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Precision@K** | yes | no | yes |
| **Recall@K** | yes | no | yes |
| **MAP@K** | yes | yes (via partial precisions) | averaged over users |
| **NDCG@K** | yes | yes (log₂ discount) | normalised to [0, 1] |

## When to pick which

- **Building a candidate generator** (first stage of a [[reco-architectures|2-step pipeline]]): optimise Recall@K with a large K (100–1000). You want the reranker to see every good candidate.
- **Final ranking shown to users**: NDCG@K with small K (5–20). It penalises bad ordering and is bounded, so cross-model comparisons are meaningful.
- **Reporting to non-ML stakeholders**: Precision@K — it's the easiest to explain ("8 of the 10 recommendations were relevant").
- **Optimising a model directly**: pair these offline metrics with a ranking-aware loss like [[bpr-loss]], because cross-entropy on individual items doesn't penalise bad ordering the way these do.

And remember: all four are **offline** metrics. They tell you a model is *probably* better. The deployment decision uses [[reco-online-metrics|online metrics]] — CTR, conversion, LTV — measured under [[ab-test|A/B test]].

---

*The source's exact framing:* The lecture introduces ranking metrics as those that "measure how relevant recommendations are for users." Precision@K is "the proportion of recommended items that are relevant — the ability of the model to label a correct sample as correct." Recall@K is "the proportion of relevant items that are recommended — the ability to capture all correct samples." NDCG@K "evaluates how well the recommender performs in recommending ranked items to users — both hit of relevant items and correctness in ranking matter." MAP@K "evaluates the average precision for each user in the dataset." [slide 4](30-Sources/Chatbots/pdf/Chatbots and Reco 04.pdf#page=4)

**Remember this:** Precision@K = hits ÷ K; Recall@K = hits ÷ |relevant|; MAP@K weights hits by partial precision (early hits matter more); NDCG@K applies a log₂(k+1) discount and normalises by the ideal ordering.

**Beyond the lecture:** the trade-offs around which metric to optimise where in a 2-step pipeline (recall for candidate generation, NDCG for final ranking), the explanation of why log₂ is the chosen discount, and the connection to BPR-loss as a ranking-aware training objective — none of that is on the slide; the slide just lists definitions.
